title: Past Simple

videoQuiz1.text: Is Joey proposing to Rachel right now?
videoQuiz1.explanation: No — the proposal is not happening right now. The conversation is about something that already happened in the past.

videoQuiz1.text: Are Ross and Rachel talking about something that happened earlier?
videoQuiz1.explanation: Yes — they are discussing a past event. Past Simple is used for actions that were completed at a specific moment in the past.

videoQuiz1.text: Look at the sentence: “You were down on one knee.”
Is Joey still on one knee right now, or is that action finished?
options: He is still on one knee
videoQuiz1.explanation: The action is finished. Past Simple describes completed actions — they started and ended in the past.

videoQuiz1.text:
Look at the dialogue:

Ross: “Did you propose to her?”
Joey: “No.”
Rachel: “Yes, you did.”
Joey: “Actually, technically, I didn’t.”
Rachel: “Well, then, why did you give me a ring?”

Are they talking about a general habit
(Joey proposes to Rachel and gives her a ring every day)
or one specific moment in the past?
options: One specific moment in the past
videoQuiz1.explanation: Past Simple refers to a specific moment in the past — not a habit or routine.

videoQuiz1.text: Look at the verb proposed in the sentence: “He got down on one knee and he proposed.”
What letters do we add to the end?
videoQuiz1.explanation: When we make a positive statement in the past, regular verbs change to the past form by adding “-ed” to the end.

videoQuiz1.text:
Look at the verbs gave and got in:
“He gave her a ring. He got down on one knee.”
Do these verbs end in “-ed”?
videoQuiz1.explanation: Irregular verbs do not follow the “-ed” rule. “Give” and “get” change their form completely: give → gave, get → got.

videoQuiz1.text:
Look at the verbs fell, went, thought, said in the dialogue:
“The ring fell on the floor. And I went down to pick it up and you thought I was proposing.”
“But you said ‘Will you marry me?’”
Do these verbs end in “-ed”?
videoQuiz1.explanation: When we make a positive statement in the past, irregular verbs change completely: fall → fell, go → went, think → thought, say → said.

grammarBank1.title: Past Simple — Positive
grammarBank1.intro:
We use the past simple for finished actions that happened in the past.

Word order: SV2 = Subject + verb in past form

Regular verbs end in ‑ed: worked, lived, proposed, travelled

Irregular verbs change form completely: give → gave   fall → fell   get → got   say → said
grammarBank1.structure: Past Simple is the same for all persons

videoQuiz1b.text: Practice — Affirmative: Choose the correct past simple form.
The ring ______ on the floor during the scene.
videoQuiz1b.explanation: Fall is irregular: fall → fell. It does not take -ed.

videoQuiz1b.text: The man ______ down to pick it up.
videoQuiz1b.explanation: Go is irregular: go → went. It does not take -ed.

videoQuiz1b.text: The woman ______ that he wanted to marry her.
videoQuiz1b.explanation: Think is irregular: think → thought. It does not take -ed.

videoQuiz1b.text: He ______ her the ring by accident.
videoQuiz1b.explanation: Give is irregular: give → gave. It does not take -ed.

videoQuiz1b.text: The other man ______ if he proposed to her.
The guy ______ down on one knee.
videoQuiz1b.explanation: Get is irregular: get → got. It does not take -ed.

videoQuiz1b.text: She ______ that he said “Will you marry me?”.
videoQuiz1b.explanation: Say is irregular: say → said. It does not take -ed.

videoQuiz1b.text: The friend ______ surprised by the news.
videoQuiz1b.explanation: Be is irregular: be → was (singular). It does not take -ed.

videoQuiz1b.text: Everything ______ so fast in that room.

videoQuiz1c.text: When Joey wants to say “he did not propose,” what word does he add to “did”?
videoQuiz1c.explanation: In negative Past Simple we use did + not (= didn't) before the infinitive.

videoQuiz1c.text: Compare two sentences: “He proposed to me” vs “No, I didn't propose to her”.
Do we add “-ed” to the verb in negative past simple sentences?
videoQuiz1c.explanation: No! In the negative form we use didn't + infinitive (base form). No “-ed” is added to the main verb.

videoQuiz1c.text:
Focus on the lines:
Ross: Did you propose to her?
Joey: I didn't propose. I did not ask her.
To ask a question about the past, what word did Ross use at the beginning of the sentence?
videoQuiz1c.explanation: In Past Simple questions we use Did as the auxiliary verb at the start of the sentence.

videoQuiz1c.text:
When we use “did” or “didn't,”
does the main verb change into the past form,
or does it stay in the base/present form?
options: It stays in the infinitives form.
It changes into the past form
videoQuiz1c.explanation: The main verb stays in the infinitive (base) form. The auxiliary “did/didn't” already shows the past tense.

videoQuiz1c.text:
Focus on the lines:
“Ross: Did you propose to her?
Joey: No
Rachel: Yes, you did.
Joey: No, I didn't.”
To agree, what shorter version does Rachel use?
videoQuiz1c.explanation: Yes, you did. — a short affirmative answer using the auxiliary “did”.

videoQuiz1c.text: To disagree, what shorter version does Joey use?
videoQuiz1c.explanation: No, I didn't. — a short negative answer using the contraction “didn't”.

blocks.intro:
We only use the past forms of verbs in positive sentences.
In negative sentences we use didn't + infinitive.
The word order in negative past simple sentences is SAI (Subject + auxiliary verb + infinitive)
Contraction: did not = didn't
The word order in interrogative past simple sentences is ASI (Auxiliary verb + subject + infinitive?)

videoQuiz1d.text: Practice — Negative: Choose the correct negative past simple form.
The man insisted that he ___ to marry her.
videoQuiz1d.explanation: Negative Past Simple: didn't + infinitive. “ask” stays in base form.

videoQuiz1d.text: Actually, he ___ to propose at all.
They ___ why he was on one knee at first.
videoQuiz1d.explanation: didn't + infinitive. “understand” stays in base form — never “understood” after didn't.

videoQuiz1d.text: The woman ___ that it was just an accident.
The man ___ the ring on purpose.
Practice — Interrogative: Choose the correct interrogative past simple form.
“___ he ___ to her?” the friend asked.
videoQuiz1d.explanation: Past Simple question: Did + subject + infinitive. The verb stays in base form.

videoQuiz1d.text: “___ you ___ me a ring?” she asked.
videoQuiz1d.explanation: Did + subject + infinitive. “give” stays in base form.

videoQuiz1d.text: “___ the ring ___ on the floor?” he explained.
videoQuiz1d.explanation: Did + subject + infinitive. “fall” stays in base form.

videoQuiz1d.text: ___ the man ___ angry during the talk?
videoQuiz1d.explanation: Did + subject + infinitive. “look” stays in base form.

videoQuiz1d.text: ___ the woman ___ shocked in the end?
videoQuiz1d.explanation: Did + subject + infinitive. “look” stays in base form.

videoQuiz2.text: Did all these events (delivering vegetables, finding the baby, giving a bath) happen at the same time, or one after another?
videoQuiz2.explanation: Past Simple is used to describe a series of actions or events occurring one after another in the past. Each action was completed before the next one began.

videoQuiz2.text: What happened first: finding the panda or giving the bath?
videoQuiz2.explanation: We use Past Simple for the series of actions and events occurring one after another.

videoQuiz2.text: Is Mr. Ping still giving the baby a bath, or is that part of the finished story?
options: Finished to give the baby the bath
still giving the baby a bath
videoQuiz2.explanation: That part of the story is finished. Past Simple always describes completed actions — the action started and ended in the past.

grammarBank2.title: Past Simple — Sequence of Actions
grammarBank2.intro: We use the past simple for finished actions that happened more than once in the past (sequence of actions).
examples: First I washed the vegetables and peeled them.
Then I chopped the carrots, sliced the cucumbers, and minced the garlic.

videoQuiz2b.text:
Mr. Ping says:
“I went out... there were no radishes... I waited.”
Did these things happen at the same time?
options: No, they happened one after another.
No, he is doing all this right now.
videoQuiz2b.explanation: Past Simple describes actions that happened one after another — not simultaneously.

videoQuiz2b.text: When the man says “I brought you inside, fed you, gave you a bath”, which action happened first?
videoQuiz2b.explanation: Bringing the panda inside came first — the actions are listed in sequence.

videoQuiz2b.text:
“I brought you inside”
→ “Fed you”
→ “Gave you a bath”
Why is Past Simple used for all these actions?
options: Because the actions are still continuing now.
Because it is a completed sequence of events in the past.
Because the order of actions doesn't matter.
videoQuiz2b.explanation: Past Simple is used because it is a completed sequence of events in the past.

videoQuiz2b.text:
When he says “I waited for someone... but no one did [come]”,
does he mean he is still waiting?
options: Yes, he is waiting for the parents to show up now.
No, the period started and finished in the past.
He plans to wait again tomorrow.
videoQuiz2b.explanation: No — the period started and finished in the past. Past Simple is always completed.

videoQuiz3.text:
Look at the sentences:
“I was a baby.”
“In the olden days you were a baby, too, Peppa.”
Do they describe an action or a quality?
videoQuiz3.explanation: We use “to be” verbs (was, were) in past form to describe qualities and characteristics like age, colour, place, identity, or a state that were true in the past.

videoQuiz3.text: Do these characteristics refer to past, present or future?
Do the “to be” verbs in past change their form according to the subjects?
videoQuiz3.explanation:
In Past Simple “to be” verbs change according to the subject:

Iwas
Youwere
He / She / Itwas
We / Theywere

videoQuiz3.text: Reorder the words to make sentences:
Put the words in correct order:
Put the words in correct order:
Do we use verbs after “was/were” in Past Simple to describe qualities and states?
videoQuiz3.explanation: We do not use verbs after was, were in Past Simple. We use other words that describe qualities and characteristics like age, colour, place, identity, or a state.

videoQuiz3.text:
Now look at negative sentences:
“No, I was not a baby.”   “And you were not a baby, too.”
Where do we put “not” in the sentence to make negative sentences?
options: before the to be verb (not was, not were)
after the to be verb (was not, were not)
videoQuiz3.explanation: We put “not” after the verb “to be” to make negatives: was not, were not.

videoQuiz3.text: Put the words in correct order:
Put the words in correct order:

Look at the dialogue:
“Were you a baby in the olden days?”
“No, I was not a baby in the olden days.”

videoQuiz3.explanation:
To make interrogative sentences with to be verbs we start the question with Was / Were.
Example: Were we babies?   Was she a baby, too?

videoQuiz3.text: Where is the subject in the question?
Put the words in correct order:
Put the words in correct order:
videoQuiz3.correct: Were you a baby in the olden days?

videoQuiz3.text: Put the words in correct order:
videoQuiz3.correct: Was she a baby in the olden days?
videoQuiz3.explanation: The word order for interrogatives with to be verbs in Past Simple is ASI = Auxiliary verb + Subject + quality words?

blocks.intro:
AFFIRMATIVE
In English, the verbs was and were are used to talk about a past state or quality. Verbs to be change according to subjects.

was — “I” and 3rd person singular
were — two or more people and “you”

NEGATIVE
We make negative sentences by adding not to “was, were”.
Contractions: was not = wasn’t   were not = weren’t

INTERROGATIVE
Questions start with Was / Were.

“was, were” are the past form of “am, is, are”

blocks.label: Pronunciation
blocks.title: Past Simple -ed Endings
blocks.intro:
The -ed ending is usually pronounced /t/ or /d/, for example booked, arrived.

We only pronounce the e in -ed when there is a t or a d before it, for example wanted, ended. In this case, -ed = /id/.

sections.heading: 1. Listen and repeat the sounds and sentences.
2. Say the past simple of these verbs. In which ones is -ed pronounced /id/?

videoQuiz3b.text: Practice — Affirmative: Choose the correct form.
Susie ___ a baby in the old photo.
The photo ___ very cute.
Peppa and Susie ___ friends many years ago too.
“It ___ me!” said Susie about the baby in the picture.
The grass in the photo ___ green.
Peppa’s dress ___ red in the olden days.
You ___ a baby too, Peppa!
The characters ___ smaller in the past.
Mommy Pig ___ at the computer.
The flowers in the garden ___ yellow.
Practice — Negative: Choose the correct form.
Peppa said: “No, I ___ a baby!”
The baby in the photo ___ Peppa.
Susie and Peppa ___ angry; they were just talking.
It ___ a new photo; it was an old one.
The parents ___ in the room at that moment.
Practice — Interrogative: Choose the correct form.
“___ I really a baby?” Peppa asked her mommy.
___ Susie a baby sheep in the picture?
___ they happy to look at old photos?
___ the photo in Susie’s hands?
___ you surprised by the old photo, Peppa?

usage: Действие произошло и закончилось в прошлом

exercises.question: Выберите правильную форму Past Simple:
options: I am going to the store.

exercises.question: Дополните: "She __ breakfast this morning."
exercises.explanation: Правильный глагол в Past Simple: eat → ate.

exercises.question: Выберите правильное предложение:
exercises.explanation: Past Simple для действия, которое произошло вчера: played.

exercises.question: Выберите отрицание в Past Simple:
exercises.explanation: Отрицание в Past Simple: did + not + базовая форма глагола.

exercises.question: Дополните отрицание: "I __ see him at the party."
Какой вопрос грамматически верен?
options: Did you went to the cinema?
Did you go to the cinema?
Did you going to the cinema?
exercises.explanation: Вопрос в Past Simple: Did + подлежащее + базовая форма глагола?

exercises.question: Завершите вопрос: "What __ she __ last weekend?"
exercises.explanation: Past Simple вопрос: Did she do...?

exercises.question: Найдите ошибку в предложении:
options: They spent all day at the beach.
She did not came to the meeting.
exercises.explanation: Ошибка: "did not came" должно быть "did not come".

exercises.question: Какой вариант верен?
options: How much money did they spent?
How much money did they spend?
How much money did spend they?
exercises.explanation: Past Simple вопрос: did + подлежащее + базовая форма (spend).

exercises.question: Какой вариант содержит ошибку?
options: They did not understand the problem.
We didn't returned home on time.

exercises.question: Выберите правильное выражение прошедшего времени:
options: They finished the project and left.
They have finished the project and left.
They finish the project and left.
They are finishing the project and leaving.
exercises.explanation: Past Simple для последовательных действий: finished...left.

exercises.question: Какой вопрос требует ответа "No, I didn't"?
exercises.explanation: "Did you call...?" требует ответа "No, I did not (didn't)."

exercises.question: Дополните: "She __ __ to Paris twice in her life."
exercises.explanation: Это Present Perfect, не Past Simple - обозначает жизненный опыт.

exercises.question: Выберите правильный порядок слов в Past Simple вопросе:
options: When did you arrive at the station?
When you did arrive at the station?
You did arrive when at the station?
When arrive did you at the station?
exercises.explanation: Past Simple вопрос: Вопросное слово + did + подлежащее + глагол?
